passages.title: The Clever Octopus
passages.text:
The octopus may look strange, but it is one of the smartest animals in the sea. Scientists have watched octopuses open jars, solve puzzles, and even escape from their tanks in aquariums.

One famous octopus named Inky lived in an aquarium in New Zealand. One night, Inky climbed out of his tank, crossed the floor, and slipped down a narrow pipe that led back to the ocean. The workers were surprised the next morning, but they were not angry. They said they did not blame him for leaving.

Why are octopuses so clever? One reason is their body. An octopus has three hearts and nine 'brains' — one in its head and one in each of its eight arms. Each arm can taste, touch, and make simple decisions on its own. This means an octopus can open a shell with one arm while looking for food with another.

Octopuses can also change the color and texture of their skin in less than a second. They do this to hide from enemies or to talk to other octopuses. Some can even copy the shape of dangerous sea animals to scare attackers away.

Scientists still have many questions about these amazing animals. What is clear is that the octopus is much more than just a strange sea creature — it is a true escape artist and a natural problem solver.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Octopuses are dangerous sea animals that attack people.
Octopuses are smart animals with amazing abilities.
How aquariums take care of octopuses.
Why octopuses have three hearts.
questions.explanation: 主旨题。全文围绕章鱼的聪明展开：会开罐子、解谜、逃跑，还解释了其身体构造和变色能力，因此选 B。其他选项都只是细节或错误信息。

questions.q: What did Inky the octopus do one night?
options: He opened a jar to get food.
He attacked the aquarium workers.
He escaped from his tank back to the ocean.
He changed color to talk to other octopuses.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段明确写道 Inky 爬出水缸、穿过地板、顺着通向大海的管子逃走，因此选 C。A 是章鱼的一般能力，并非 Inky 那晚做的事。

questions.q: The word 'narrow' in Paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to ______.
options: wide
thin
long
deep
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。章鱼能从管道逃走，管子应该是'狭窄的'，narrow 意为'窄的'，与 thin 意思最接近。wide 是反义词。

questions.q: According to the passage, an octopus's arms can ______.
options: taste and touch things by themselves
only follow orders from the head
grow back into new octopuses
see in the dark
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说每条腕里有独立的'脑'，可以自己尝味道、感知触觉并做简单决定，因此选 A。B 与原文相反。

questions.q: What can we infer about the aquarium workers in New Zealand?
options: They wanted to sell Inky to another zoo.
They understood and even respected Inky's escape.
They were fired because Inky escaped.
They built a bigger tank for Inky.
questions.explanation: 推断题。文中说工作人员'不生气，也不怪他离开'，可推断他们理解甚至佩服 Inky 的出逃行为，选 B。其他选项文中均无依据。

passages.title: The Camera in Your Pocket
passages.text:
Twenty years ago, taking a good photo was not easy. People used cameras with film, and they could only take about 36 photos at a time. After that, they had to wait several days for a shop to develop the film. If a photo was bad, it was too late to take it again.

Today, almost everyone carries a powerful camera in their pocket — the smartphone. A modern phone can take thousands of photos, and you can see the result at once. If you don't like a photo, you simply delete it and try again.

But the changes are bigger than just numbers. Phone cameras use clever software, not just lenses. When you take a picture at night, the phone actually takes many photos in a second and joins the best parts together. That is why night photos from a phone can look bright and clear.

Smartphones have also changed who becomes a photographer. In the past, you needed expensive cameras and special training. Now, a student with a phone can take a photo that millions of people see online. Many young people have started careers by posting their photos and videos on social media.

However, some people worry that we take too many photos and look at real life through a screen. Perhaps the best lesson is this: use the camera in your pocket to remember life, but don't forget to enjoy the moment with your own eyes first.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How to become a professional photographer
How smartphones have changed photography and our lives
The history of film cameras
Why people should stop taking photos
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章对比了胶片相机时代与智能手机时代，介绍手机拍照的技术原理及带来的影响，核心是'智能手机如何改变摄影和生活'，选 B。

questions.q: With a film camera twenty years ago, people ______.
options: could see their photos right away
could take thousands of photos at a time
had to wait days to see their photos
never made bad photos
questions.explanation: 细节题。第一段说胶片相机一次只能拍约 36 张，还要等好几天冲洗才能看到照片，因此选 C。A、B 都是现代手机的特点。

questions.q: The phrase 'develop the film' in Paragraph 1 probably means ______.
options: to buy a new camera
to make the photos from the film ready to see
to throw the film away
to take more photos
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。结合上下文'拍完要等好几天'，develop the film 指'冲洗胶片'，即把胶卷变成可以看的照片，选 B。

questions.q: Why do night photos from a phone look bright and clear?
options: Because phones have very large lenses.
Because the phone combines the best parts of many quick photos.
Because people use special lights at night.
Because night photos are easier to take than day photos.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段明确说手机在夜间会快速拍摄多张照片，然后把最好的部分拼合在一起，所以夜景照片明亮清晰，选 B。

questions.q: What does the writer suggest at the end of the passage?
options: We should stop using phone cameras.
Everyone should become an online photographer.
We should enjoy real moments, not just photograph them.
Old film cameras are better than smartphones.
questions.explanation: 推断题。最后一段作者提醒人们别只透过屏幕看生活，要'先用自己的眼睛享受当下'，可推断作者建议我们不要只顾拍照而忽略真实体验，选 C。

passages.title: Esports: More Than Just Playing Games
passages.text:
When your parents say 'Stop playing games!', you can now tell them that playing games is a real job for some people. Esports, or electronic sports, means playing video games in organized competitions. The best players in the world train for many hours every day, just like football players or swimmers.

Esports has grown very fast. Big tournaments now fill whole stadiums with fans, and millions more watch online. In 2022, esports was even part of the Asian Games as an official medal sport. Top players can win prize money worth millions of dollars, and many of them are only in their late teens or early twenties.

But being a pro gamer is not easy. Players usually train eight to twelve hours a day. They practise aiming, learn team strategies, and watch videos of their matches to find mistakes. They also need to exercise their bodies, because quick hands need a healthy body. Many teams have coaches, doctors, and even cooks.

Esports also teaches useful skills. Players must communicate clearly, make decisions in a second, and stay calm under pressure. These skills help in school and in many jobs.

However, balance is important. Doctors warn that playing too much can hurt your eyes, your back, and your sleep. The lesson from real pro players is surprising: they succeed not by playing all day, but by training smart, resting well, and working with their team. That is a game plan everyone can learn from.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of this passage?
options: To teach readers how to win video game tournaments
To explain what esports is and what we can learn from it
To warn parents about the dangers of all video games
To describe the history of the Asian Games
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章介绍了电竞的定义、发展、职业选手的训练方式以及能学到的技能，核心是让读者了解电竞并从中获得启示，选 B。

questions.q: According to the passage, in 2022 esports ______.
options: was played at the Olympic Games
became an official medal sport at the Asian Games
was banned in many countries
gave every player a million dollars
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说 2022 年电竞成为亚运会正式奖牌项目，选 B。A 错在是亚运会而非奥运会；D 夸大事实，是'顶尖选手'才能赢得高额奖金。

questions.q: The word 'strategies' in Paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to ______.
options: plans
rules
secrets
skills
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。strategy 意为'策略、打法'，与 plans（计划、方案）最接近，选 A。skills 是'技巧'，侧重操作能力，而非战术安排。

questions.q: Why do esports teams have doctors and cooks?
options: Because players often get sick from computers.
Because players need a healthy body to play well.
Because tournaments last for many months.
Because the rules of esports require them.
questions.explanation: 细节/推断题。第三段说'敏捷的双手需要健康的身体'，选手每天训练 8-12 小时，所以队伍配备队医和厨师来保障健康，选 B。

questions.q: What can we infer about successful pro gamers?
options: They play games all day without rest.
They do not need to communicate with teammates.
They succeed through smart training and good teamwork.
They are all over thirty years old.
questions.explanation: 推断题。末段点明真正的职业选手'不是靠整天玩，而是靠聪明训练、好好休息和团队协作'成功，选 C。A、B 与原文相反；D 与第二段'大多不到二十岁'矛盾。

passages.title: Robots on the Red Planet
passages.text:
Mars is called the Red Planet because its soil is full of red dust. It is cold, dry, and very far away — it takes a spacecraft about seven months to get there from Earth. No human has ever visited Mars, but we have already sent something clever: robots.

These robot explorers are called rovers. A rover looks a bit like a small car with six wheels, cameras for eyes, and a long robot arm. Because radio signals take up to twenty minutes to travel from Earth to Mars, a rover cannot wait for orders all the time. It must make some decisions by itself, such as avoiding rocks and choosing safe paths.

In 2021, NASA's rover Perseverance landed on Mars. Its job is to look for signs of ancient life. Scientists believe that billions of years ago, Mars had rivers and lakes. Perseverance landed in an old lake area, where it collects rock samples. One day, another spacecraft may bring these samples back to Earth so scientists can study them in labs.

Perseverance also carried a small helicopter named Ingenuity. It was the first machine to fly on another planet. Flying on Mars is hard because the air is very thin, but Ingenuity flew more than 70 times — far more than anyone expected.

Why explore Mars at all? Scientists say it helps us understand our own planet better, and it prepares us for the day when humans finally take the long journey there. Perhaps someone reading this article today will be one of them.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How humans live on Mars today
Robot explorers on Mars and what they do
Why Mars is called the Red Planet
How to build a helicopter
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章介绍了火星探测车（rover）的构造与自主决策能力、毅力号的任务以及机智号直升机，核心是'火星上的机器人探索者'，选 B。C 只是开头一个细节。

questions.q: A rover on Mars must make some decisions by itself because ______.
options: it is smarter than any scientist
radio signals between Earth and Mars are too slow
scientists are too busy to control it
it wants to explore freely
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说无线电信号从地球到火星最长要 20 分钟，探测车不能一直等待指令，所以必须自己避开岩石、选择路线，选 B。

questions.q: The word 'ancient' in Paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to ______.
options: modern
very old
foreign
tiny
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。ancient 意为'古代的、久远的'。文中说数十亿年前火星有河湖，毅力号寻找'远古生命'的迹象，very old 意思最接近，选 B。

questions.q: Why was flying Ingenuity on Mars difficult?
options: Because Mars is too far from the sun.
Because the air on Mars is very thin.
Because the helicopter was too heavy.
Because there are too many rocks.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第四段明确说'在火星上飞行很难，因为空气非常稀薄'，选 B。

questions.q: What can we infer from the last paragraph?
options: Humans will never travel to Mars.
Exploring Mars may help future human journeys there.
Only scientists can read this article.
Mars exploration is a waste of money.
questions.explanation: 推断题。末段说探索火星能帮助我们了解地球，并'为人类最终踏上火星的那一天做准备'，可推断火星探测是在为未来载人登陆铺路，选 B。A、D 与原文态度相反。

passages.title: Turning the Tide on Plastic
passages.text:
Every year, millions of tonnes of plastic flow into the ocean. Plastic bags, bottles, and wrappers do not disappear; they slowly break into tiny pieces called microplastics, which are now found in fish, in sea salt, and even in drinking water. Scientists warn that if nothing changes, by 2050 the ocean could contain more plastic than fish, measured by weight.

The problem begins on land. Much of the plastic in the sea comes from rivers, especially in places where rubbish collection is weak. Once plastic reaches the ocean, currents carry it across the world. Sea turtles mistake floating bags for jellyfish, their favourite food, and birds feed plastic pieces to their chicks, believing they are fish.

But the story is not hopeless, and some of the most interesting solutions come from young people. Boyan Slat, a Dutch teenager, was diving in Greece when he noticed more plastic bags than fish. Instead of just complaining, he designed a system of floating barriers that use ocean currents to collect plastic. His idea grew into The Ocean Cleanup, a project that has already removed millions of kilograms of rubbish from the Pacific. He started working on it when he was only sixteen.

Technology alone, however, cannot solve everything. Experts agree that the cheapest plastic is the one we never produce. Many countries now charge for plastic bags, and some have banned single-use items like straws and cutlery. Scientists are also developing new materials from plants, such as seaweed packaging, that break down naturally in weeks instead of centuries.

What can one student do? More than you might think. Carrying a reusable bottle, refusing a plastic straw, and joining a beach clean-up all matter. When millions of people make small choices, the tide can truly turn. The ocean has supported life on Earth for billions of years; perhaps it is time for our generation to return the favour.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Plastic pollution is too big a problem to solve.
The ocean plastic problem is serious, but people are finding ways to fight it.
Boyan Slat became rich by cleaning the ocean.
Fish in the ocean are all dying from plastic.
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章前半讲海洋塑料污染的严重性，后半转向青少年发明、政策和新材料等解决办法，并以'我们能做什么'结尾，整体是'问题严峻但有人正在应对'，选 B。A 与文章乐观基调相反。

questions.q: According to the passage, most ocean plastic originally comes from ______.
options: ships that dump rubbish at sea
rivers carrying waste from the land
broken fishing nets in the Pacific
beaches in rich countries
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说'海里的大部分塑料来自河流'，尤其是垃圾收集薄弱地区，即从陆地经河流入海，选 B。

questions.q: The word 'barriers' in Paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to ______.
options: walls that block or guide things
machines that burn rubbish
boats that carry tourists
nets used for catching fish
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。barrier 原意为'障碍物、屏障'。文中指利用海流收集塑料的漂浮'围栏/拦截装置'，与'阻挡或引导物体的墙状物'最接近，选 A。

questions.q: Why do experts say 'the cheapest plastic is the one we never produce'?
options: Because making plastic costs too much money.
Because stopping plastic at the source is better than cleaning it up later.
Because plant materials are free to produce.
Because people refuse to buy plastic products.
questions.explanation: 推断题。第四段先说'仅靠技术无法解决一切'，随后举例限塑政策，可见这句话的意思是'从源头减少塑料比事后清理更划算、更有效'，选 B。

questions.q: What can we infer about the writer's attitude toward young people?
options: They are too young to understand environmental problems.
They should leave the problem to scientists and governments.
Their choices and ideas can make a real difference.
They cause most of the plastic pollution.
questions.explanation: 推断题。作者用 16 岁少年 Boyan Slat 的事迹作正面例子，结尾又说'一个学生能做的比想象的多''我们这一代是时候回报海洋了'，可见作者相信年轻人的选择和创意能带来真正改变，选 C。

passages.title: The Great Library That Vanished
passages.text:
Imagine a library that tried to collect every book in the world. More than two thousand years ago, such a place really existed: the Great Library of Alexandria, in Egypt. At its height, it may have held hundreds of thousands of scrolls — the books of the ancient world, made from long rolls of papyrus paper.

The library was more than a storage room. It was a research centre where the greatest minds of the age lived and worked. Scholars received free meals, a salary, and even freedom from taxes, so they could focus completely on study. It was there that a man named Eratosthenes calculated the size of the Earth with surprising accuracy, using only shadows, a well, and basic geometry — all without leaving Egypt.

The library's collectors were famously determined. Ships arriving at Alexandria's port were searched, and any scrolls found on board were taken to the library to be copied. The copy was returned to the owner, while the library kept the original. In this way, knowledge from Greece, India, and beyond flowed into one city.

So what happened to this wonder? The honest answer is that nobody knows for sure. Fires during wars, including one linked to Julius Caesar in 48 BC, damaged parts of the collection. Later emperors cut funding, and over the following centuries the library slowly faded away rather than dying in one dramatic blaze. Its disappearance remains one of history's great mysteries, which is why writers still argue about it today.

Yet the library's true legacy was never the building. It was the idea that knowledge belongs to everyone, and that collecting, copying, and sharing ideas makes civilisation stronger. Every time you search for information on the internet in seconds, you are enjoying a dream that began in Alexandria — a single place where all human knowledge could live together.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How Julius Caesar destroyed the Great Library
The Great Library of Alexandria: its work, its end, and its lasting idea
How ancient Egyptians made papyrus paper
The life of the scientist Eratosthenes
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章依次介绍亚历山大图书馆的规模与运作、它的消失之谜，最后升华到'知识共享'的精神遗产，选 B。A、C、D 都只是局部细节。

questions.q: What did the library do with scrolls found on ships?
options: It returned them immediately to the owners.
It sold them to pay for new buildings.
It copied them and kept the originals.
It burned them to protect local knowledge.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说进港船只上的书卷会被拿去抄写，抄本还给主人，原件留在图书馆，选 C。

questions.q: The word 'vanished' in the title is closest in meaning to ______.
options: disappeared
burned
moved
survived
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。vanish 意为'消失'。第四段说图书馆是在数百年间慢慢'消逝'的，标题中的 vanished 即 disappeared，选 A。burned 只是可能原因之一，且不准确。

questions.q: Why does the writer say nobody knows for sure how the library ended?
options: Because all records were destroyed in a single fire.
Because it declined slowly over centuries through several causes.
Because historians are not interested in the library.
Because the library never really existed.
questions.explanation: 细节/推断题。第四段列举了战火、经费削减等原因，并强调图书馆是'在数百年间逐渐衰落'而非毁于一场大火，所以结局成谜，选 B。

questions.q: According to the writer, what is the library's true legacy?
options: The scrolls that survived the fires
The idea that knowledge should be collected and shared by all
The buildings tourists can visit today
The salary system for scholars
questions.explanation: 细节/推断题。末段明确说图书馆真正的遗产'从来不是建筑，而是知识属于所有人、收集与分享使文明更强大的理念'，选 B。

passages.title: Eat Like an Athlete
passages.text:
What do professional footballers eat before a big match? The answer might disappoint fans of fast food: no burgers, no fries, and no sugary drinks. Top athletes treat food as fuel, and sports scientists now know that the right diet can be worth as much as extra hours of training.

The basic idea is simple. Athletes need three things from food. First, carbohydrates — foods like rice, bread, and pasta — provide quick energy for muscles. Second, protein, found in eggs, fish, and beans, repairs the small damage that hard exercise causes in the body. Third, water and minerals keep everything running; losing just two percent of your body's water can noticeably slow your reactions, which matters in sports and even in esports.

Timing matters as much as ingredients. Eating a huge meal right before exercise is a mistake, because the body sends blood to the stomach instead of the muscles. Most athletes eat a light, carbohydrate-rich meal two to three hours before competing, and a small protein snack afterwards to help muscles recover. Many professional clubs now employ full-time chefs who design menus for each player.

Interestingly, the same rules apply to the brain. Students often reach for energy drinks and sweets before exams, but sugar gives a short burst of energy followed by a crash, leaving you more tired than before. A breakfast with eggs, whole-grain bread, and fruit releases energy slowly and steadily — exactly what a three-hour exam requires. Studies have found that students who eat breakfast regularly score better on memory tests than those who skip it.

You do not need a club chef to eat well. The lesson from sports science is available to everyone: colourful vegetables, enough protein, regular water, and sensible timing. Whether your battlefield is a football pitch, a gaming arena, or a maths exam, your plate is part of your training.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Professional athletes never eat fast food.
Eating like an athlete can improve both sports performance and study.
Energy drinks are good for students before exams.
Football clubs should hire more chefs.
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章从运动员饮食讲到大脑与考试，结论是'吃对了，运动和学习都受益'，选 B。A、D 是细节；C 与第四段相反。

questions.q: According to the passage, protein is important because it ______.
options: provides quick energy for muscles
repairs damage caused by hard exercise
keeps the body cool during matches
makes athletes feel less hungry
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说蛋白质能修复剧烈运动给身体造成的微小损伤，选 B。A 是碳水化合物的作用。

questions.q: The word 'crash' in Paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to ______.
options: a sudden feeling of tiredness after energy drops
a car accident caused by fast driving
a loud noise during a football match
a quick improvement in exam scores
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。文中说糖分带来短暂能量后紧跟着 crash，'让你比之前更累'，可推断 crash 指能量骤降后的疲惫感，选 A。

questions.q: Why do most athletes eat two to three hours before competing?
options: Because they are too nervous to eat later.
Because eating right before exercise sends blood away from the muscles.
Because matches always start early in the morning.
Because coaches want to save money on food.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说运动前大吃会让血液流向胃部而非肌肉，所以运动员提前 2-3 小时吃清淡的一餐，选 B。

questions.q: What can we infer about students who skip breakfast?
options: They always fail their exams.
They may do worse on memory tests than students who eat breakfast.
They prefer to study late at night.
They do not like eggs and bread.
questions.explanation: 推断题。第四段说研究发现规律吃早餐的学生在记忆测试中成绩更好，反向可推断不吃早餐的学生可能表现较差，选 B。A 说得过于绝对，'总是不及格'没有依据。

passages.title: The New Kid Who Spoke Up
passages.text:
Moving to a new school in the middle of the year is hard for anyone, and for Lucas, a fourteen-year-old who had just arrived from another city, the first weeks were painfully quiet. He sat at the back of the class, ate lunch alone, and answered questions in a voice so soft that teachers often asked him to repeat himself.

The change began with a broken drone. A group of boys in his class were arguing in the playground because their drone would not take off. Lucas, who had spent two years building model aircraft with his grandfather, walked over and pointed out that one propeller was on backwards. Thirty seconds later, the drone was in the air, and the boys were looking at him as if he had performed magic.

That afternoon, someone invited Lucas to sit with them at lunch. Someone else asked if he wanted to join the school's new robotics club. Lucas almost said no — saying no was his habit, a wall he had built to protect himself from disappointment. But then he remembered his grandfather's advice: 'A plane that never leaves the ground is safe, but that is not what planes are for.' He said yes.

The robotics club did not magically fix everything. At the first meeting, Lucas's suggestion for the team project was rejected, and he went home feeling like the new kid all over again. His team leader, however, explained why the idea would not work and asked him to design the robot's arm instead — the hardest part. By the end of the term, their robot won second prize at a city competition, and Lucas gave the thank-you speech in front of two hundred people. His voice shook for the first sentence. By the last sentence, it did not.

Looking back, Lucas says the bravest thing he ever did was not building a robot or giving a speech. It was walking across a playground to help some strangers with a broken drone. Courage, he learned, is not the absence of fear; it is deciding that something else matters more.

questions.q: What is the main lesson of the story?
options: Robotics clubs are the best way to win prizes.
Being brave means taking small steps even when you feel afraid.
New students should stay quiet for the first few weeks.
Grandparents give the best advice about drones.
questions.explanation: 主旨题。故事通过 Lucas 从孤僻到融入的经历，结尾点题'勇气不是不害怕，而是认定有更重要的事'，即带着恐惧迈出小步，选 B。

questions.q: How did Lucas first connect with his classmates?
options: He gave a speech at a city competition.
He joined their football game at lunch.
He fixed their drone that would not take off.
He helped them with their maths homework.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说 Lucas 指出无人机螺旋桨装反了，修好后同学们对他刮目相看，这是他与同学建立联系的开始，选 C。

questions.q: In Paragraph 3, saying no is described as 'a wall'. What does this mean?
options: Lucas sat behind a real wall at lunch.
Lucas used refusal to protect himself from being hurt.
Lucas was physically unable to join clubs.
Lucas wanted to build a wall around the school.
questions.explanation: 词义猜测/推断题。'wall'是比喻，原文紧跟解释'用来保护自己不受失望的伤害'，即'拒绝'是 Lucas 自我保护的屏障，选 B。

questions.q: What happened after Lucas's first idea was rejected by the club?
options: He quit the club and never went back.
The team leader gave him the hardest design task instead.
He argued with the team leader angrily.
His grandfather came to school to help him.
questions.explanation: 细节题。第四段说队长解释了方案不可行的原因，并请他改去设计最难的部分——机械臂，选 B。A 与后文矛盾，他没有退出。

questions.q: What does the grandfather's advice about planes really mean?
options: Planes are dangerous and should stay on the ground.
Staying safe is more important than trying new things.
People, like planes, are meant to take risks and grow.
Lucas should become a pilot when he grows up.
questions.explanation: 推断题。'永不离地的飞机很安全，但那不是飞机的用途'是比喻，暗示人不应因害怕失败而拒绝尝试，成长需要冒险，选 C。这正是 Lucas 决定答应加入社团的原因。
